content.text: Reusing proven reasoning structures across similar problems is a core efficiency lever in agent systems.

stores distilled thought templates — validated reasoning scaffolds abstracted from past successful runs — and retrieves them at inference time so the LLM receives a structured scaffold rather than an empty prompt. The result is higher accuracy on complex tasks with lower trial-and-error cost per call.

Buffer of Thoughts is a prompting and orchestration paradigm introduced in the 2024 paper “BoT: Thought Template” from Peking University. The central claim is that many problem classes share underlying reasoning

even when the surface details differ. An agent debugging a rate-limit error and an agent debugging a timeout error both need the same diagnostic scaffold: identify the error code, classify it, choose a retry strategy, and verify the fix.

Rather than re-deriving that scaffold from scratch on every call, BoT maintains a library of such templates indexed by problem type. At runtime a retriever selects the most relevant template, an instantiator binds it to the current problem context, and the LLM reasons within that structure. After a successful run a distiller extracts any new reusable scaffold and writes it back to the buffer, making the system self-improving over time.
Critically, templates are not full solved examples (few-shot) — they are
of how to reason, not the solved answer. This makes them far more generalizable than cached responses and more token-efficient than large few-shot banks.

Standard Chain-of-Thought prompting leaves the reasoning structure entirely to the LLM, which produces inconsistent results across similar tasks. On first encounter with a problem class, the model may reason well; on a slightly different instantiation it may take a suboptimal path or get lost in irrelevant details.

This inconsistency is costly in production agent systems. A customer-support bot that handles thousands of billing disputes per day cannot afford to rediscover the correct diagnostic sequence on each ticket. A code-generation agent that repeatedly forgets to validate input types wastes tokens on incorrect drafts. The pain is compounded because the
reasoning pattern was already discovered on a previous run — it just wasn't stored anywhere.

Buffer of Thoughts converts this wasted inference into institutional memory. Once a high-quality reasoning path is distilled, every future call in the same problem class benefits from it. This is analogous to how senior engineers write runbooks: the hard-won diagnostic logic is captured once and reused by all.

Beyond individual task quality, BoT addresses a scaling challenge. As agents handle a wider problem surface, the effort needed to maintain per-task system prompts grows linearly. A meta-buffer with good retrieval makes the marginal cost of handling a new problem class much lower — the new template is authored once and the rest of the system adapts automatically.
Empirically, BoT improves accuracy by 11–27 % over standard CoT on mathematical reasoning and code synthesis benchmarks by anchoring the LLM to a validated reasoning path.
A reasoning scaffold written once is reused across all instances of a problem class, making prompt engineering investment compound rather than dissipate.
Successful runs feed new templates back into the buffer, so quality improves over time without manual intervention.
A scaffold compresses what would otherwise require a long few-shot bank into a compact structural description, reducing token usage per call.
Templates make the agent's reasoning strategy explicit and auditable — a reviewer can inspect which scaffold was used and why a particular reasoning path was chosen.
Parallel agent workers all retrieve from the same buffer, producing structurally consistent reasoning across thousands of concurrent tasks.
The meta-buffer needs indexing, versioning, pruning of stale templates, and score maintenance. This is non-trivial infrastructure that adds operational complexity.
An embedding-based retrieval step adds 10–80 ms per call (depending on buffer size and vector DB). For real-time applications this matters.
A new deployment has an empty buffer. Early runs fall back to standard CoT until enough templates have been distilled, which can take hundreds of calls.
Templates authored for one API version or task context may become stale as the environment changes. Without active maintenance the buffer can guide the LLM toward outdated strategies.

If the retriever selects a wrong template (false-positive match), the LLM follows a scaffold that is structurally wrong for the problem, often producing a confidently wrong answer — harder to catch than an empty-prompt failure.
Highly novel tasks have no relevant template; BoT offers no benefit and adds latency. Creative or open-ended generation tasks gain little from structured scaffolds.
The Buffer of Thoughts pipeline has five conceptual stages that can be implemented in a synchronous chain or as an async pipeline with background distillation.
in the meta-buffer and returns the top-
matches. The scoring function is typically:
is a tunable decay rate, typically 0.01 per day).

: problem-specific variables (entity names, error codes, API endpoints) are bound into the template's placeholder slots. This produces a concrete structured prompt with the reasoning steps pre-specified but the details filled in for the current problem.

The LLM receives the instantiated prompt and generates a solution by following the scaffold. Because the high-level structure is given, the model can focus its capacity on the object-level content rather than figuring out the reasoning strategy. This is why BoT improves accuracy most strongly on tasks with a well-defined solution structure.

An optional judge (another LLM call, a unit test, or a symbolic checker) scores the solution. This score feeds the distillation decision: only high-quality solutions (above a configurable threshold) contribute new templates to the buffer.
A distiller LLM call (or heuristic) reads the problem-solution pair and extracts the
reasoning structure, stripping problem-specific details. The resulting scaffold is stored in the meta-buffer with an initial score equal to the judge's rating.
The diagram below shows the full runtime architecture, including the self-improving feedback loop from distillation back into the meta-buffer.

attrs.alt: Buffer of Thoughts runtime architecture showing meta-buffer, retriever, instantiator, LLM reasoner, distiller, and feedback loop

content.text: Tasks with recurring problem classes: customer support triage, API error handling, code review checklists, data validation pipelines.
Agent systems where evals show high variance across similar inputs — BoT reduces variance by anchoring reasoning structure.
Long-running agent deployments where buffer quality improves over weeks, compounding the return on initial scaffold authoring effort.
Multi-agent systems where consistency across parallel workers is important.
One-off or highly novel tasks with no precedent in the buffer. The cold-start penalty is paid for no benefit.
Creative tasks (story generation, brainstorming) where structural scaffolds constrain rather than guide.
Latency-critical applications where the retrieval round-trip cannot be tolerated.
Simple, single-step tasks where CoT already achieves near-ceiling accuracy. The marginal gain does not justify the overhead.
A production BoT implementation has four components: the
. Below is a recommended build order.

Start with 5–10 hand-authored templates covering the most common problem classes. Tag each with domain keywords. Do not wait for the self-improvement loop to populate the buffer — early templates are the foundation.
Before investing in embeddings, validate the pipeline end-to-end with simple tag overlap scoring. This ships faster and reveals integration issues.
Once the pipeline is stable, replace tag scoring with cosine similarity over a vector store. Use a lightweight embedding model (e.g., text-embedding-3-small) to keep latency low.
Run the distiller as a background task after each successful call so it never adds to response latency.
Implement a nightly job that recalculates decayed scores and removes templates whose decayed score falls below a threshold (e.g., 0.3).
Log which template was retrieved for each problem and periodically audit mismatches. Poor retrieval is the most common failure mode.
See the code examples below for concrete implementations at each stage of this build order.
A self-contained Buffer of Thoughts with tag-based retrieval, template storage, and a mock LLM call. Runnable with Python 3.10 or later, no dependencies.

"""
Minimal Buffer of Thoughts implementation.
Each "thought template" is a dict with a problem description,
a reasoning scaffold, and a quality score.
"""
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ThoughtTemplate:
    id: str
    description: str          # what problem class this covers
    scaffold: str             # the reasoning structure / prompt fragment
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    score: float = 1.0        # updated after each use
    uses: int = 0

class MetaBuffer:
    def __init__(self):
        self._store: list[ThoughtTemplate] = []

    def add(self, template: ThoughtTemplate) -> None:
        self._store.append(template)

    def retrieve(self, query_tags: list[str], top_k: int = 1) -> list[ThoughtTemplate]:
        """Simple tag-overlap retrieval (replace with embedding search in prod)."""
        scored = []
        for t in self._store:
            overlap = len(set(query_tags) & set(t.tags))
            if overlap > 0:
                scored.append((overlap * t.score, t))
        scored.sort(key=lambda x: -x[0])
        return [t for _, t in scored[:top_k]]

# ---- populate a small buffer ----
buffer = MetaBuffer()

buffer.add(ThoughtTemplate(
    id="tpl-001",
    description="Diagnose and recover from HTTP 4xx/5xx API errors",
    scaffold=(
        "1. Identify the error code and response body.\n"
        "2. Classify: client error (4xx) or server error (5xx).\n"
        "3. For 429: extract Retry-After header; back-off exponentially.\n"
        "4. For 5xx: retry up to 3 times with jitter.\n"
        "5. If unrecoverable, surface a human-readable message."
    ),
    tags=["api", "error", "retry", "http"],
    score=0.92,
))

buffer.add(ThoughtTemplate(
    id="tpl-002",
    description="Decompose a multi-step user request into a plan",
    scaffold=(
        "1. Extract the end goal from the user request.\n"
        "2. Identify sub-goals required to reach it.\n"
        "3. Order sub-goals by dependency.\n"
        "4. For each sub-goal, select the appropriate tool or action.\n"
        "5. Verify feasibility before executing."
    ),
    tags=["planning", "decomposition", "agent"],
    score=0.88,
))

# ---- use the buffer ----
def reason_with_buffer(problem: str, tags: list[str], call_llm) -> str:
    templates = buffer.retrieve(tags)
    if templates:
        tpl = templates[0]
        prompt = (
            f"Problem: {problem}\n\n"
            f"Use this reasoning scaffold:\n{tpl.scaffold}\n\n"
            "Now reason step-by-step and produce the solution."
        )
        tpl.uses += 1
    else:
        prompt = f"Problem: {problem}\n\nReason step-by-step."
    return call_llm(prompt)

# Simulated LLM call for demonstration
def mock_llm(prompt: str) -> str:
    return f"[LLM response to {len(prompt)}-char prompt]"

result = reason_with_buffer(
    problem="The payment API is returning 429 errors under load.",
    tags=["api", "error", "http"],
    call_llm=mock_llm,
)
print(result)
Example 2 — Production implementation with async distillation
An embedding-based implementation with score decay, async distillation, and a pluggable embed function. Replace the mock stubs with your vector DB and LLM client.

"""
Production-grade Buffer of Thoughts:
- Embedding-based retrieval with cosine similarity
- Async background distillation
- Score decay for staleness management
"""
import asyncio, math, time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Awaitable

@dataclass
class ThoughtTemplate:
    id: str
    description: str
    scaffold: str
    tags: list[str]
    embedding: list[float] = field(default_factory=list)
    score: float = 1.0
    uses: int = 0
    last_used: float = field(default_factory=time.time)

    def decayed_score(self, decay_rate: float = 0.01) -> float:
        age_days = (time.time() - self.last_used) / 86400
        return self.score * math.exp(-decay_rate * age_days)

class EmbeddingMetaBuffer:
    def __init__(self, embed_fn: Callable[[str], list[float]]):
        self._store: list[ThoughtTemplate] = []
        self._embed = embed_fn

    def add(self, tpl: ThoughtTemplate) -> None:
        if not tpl.embedding:
            tpl.embedding = self._embed(tpl.description + " " + " ".join(tpl.tags))
        self._store.append(tpl)

    def _cosine(self, a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
        dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
        na = math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in a))
        nb = math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in b))
        return dot / (na * nb + 1e-9)

    def retrieve(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[ThoughtTemplate]:
        qe = self._embed(query)
        ranked = sorted(
            self._store,
            key=lambda t: self._cosine(qe, t.embedding) * t.decayed_score(),
            reverse=True,
        )
        return ranked[:top_k]

    async def distill_and_store(
        self, problem: str, solution: str, quality_score: float,
        distill_fn: Callable[[str, str], Awaitable[str]],
    ) -> "ThoughtTemplate | None":
        if quality_score < 0.7:
            return None
        scaffold = await distill_fn(problem, solution)
        tpl = ThoughtTemplate(
            id=f"distilled-{int(time.time())}",
            description=f"Auto-distilled from: {problem[:60]}",
            scaffold=scaffold, tags=[], score=quality_score,
        )
        self.add(tpl)
        return tpl

async def bot_run(problem, buffer, llm, judge, distill):
    templates = buffer.retrieve(problem)
    if templates:
        scaffolds = "\n\n".join(
            f"[{t.description}]\n{t.scaffold}" for t in templates
        )
        prompt = f"Problem: {problem}\n\nScaffolds:\n{scaffolds}\n\nSolve step by step."
        for t in templates:
            t.uses += 1; t.last_used = time.time()
    else:
        prompt = f"Problem: {problem}\nSolve step by step."
    solution = await llm(prompt)
    score = await judge(problem, solution)
    asyncio.create_task(buffer.distill_and_store(problem, solution, score, distill))
    return solution

# ---- stubs for local testing ----
def mock_embed(text):
    import hashlib
    return [b / 255.0 for b in hashlib.md5(text.encode()).digest()]

async def main():
    buf = EmbeddingMetaBuffer(mock_embed)
    buf.add(ThoughtTemplate(
        id="seed-001", description="API error diagnosis",
        scaffold="1. Check status.\n2. Parse error.\n3. Decide retry.",
        tags=["api", "error"], score=0.9,
    ))
    result = await bot_run(
        "The search API returns 503 intermittently.", buf,
        llm=lambda p: asyncio.sleep(0) or "solution",
        judge=lambda p, s: asyncio.sleep(0) or 0.85,
        distill=lambda p, s: asyncio.sleep(0) or "1. Check.\n2. Fix.",
    )
    print(result, f"| buffer size: {len(buf._store)}")

asyncio.run(main())

A customer-support automation platform handling 50,000 tickets per day across three categories (billing, technical, account) uses BoT to standardize agent reasoning. Without BoT, the agent's resolution quality varied significantly within each category — the same billing dispute was handled differently depending on how the customer phrased the ticket.

After seeding the buffer with one scaffold per category and enabling async distillation, the platform observed a 19 % reduction in escalation rate over four weeks as the buffer accumulated higher-quality templates from successful resolutions. The retrieval step added ~40 ms latency (pgvector on PostgreSQL), which was acceptable for asynchronous ticket handling.

"""
Real-world scenario: customer-support bot that retrieves reasoning scaffolds
from a meta-buffer before responding to tickets.
"""
import os, asyncio, json, time, math
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

# In production, replace stubs with real Anthropic / OpenAI calls and
# a vector DB (Pinecone, pgvector, Chroma, Weaviate).

@dataclass
class Ticket:
    id: str
    category: str       # "billing" | "technical" | "account"
    text: str

TEMPLATES = {
    "billing": (
        "1. Confirm the charge date and amount from the billing system.\n"
        "2. Check if the customer has an active subscription or one-time purchase.\n"
        "3. Identify the discrepancy (double-charge, wrong tier, promo not applied).\n"
        "4. Issue refund or adjustment per policy; log in CRM."
    ),
    "technical": (
        "1. Reproduce the reported error with the customer's account ID.\n"
        "2. Check service health dashboard and recent deploy log.\n"
        "3. If known issue: link to status page and ETA.\n"
        "4. If unknown: escalate to Tier 2 with diagnostics."
    ),
    "account": (
        "1. Verify identity via email + last-4 of payment method.\n"
        "2. Check account status (active / suspended / deleted).\n"
        "3. Apply requested change (email, password reset, MFA toggle).\n"
        "4. Confirm change by email and log action."
    ),
}

async def handle_ticket(ticket: Ticket, call_llm) -> dict:
    scaffold = TEMPLATES.get(ticket.category, "Reason step by step.")
    prompt = (
        f"Support ticket #{ticket.id} [{ticket.category}]:\n{ticket.text}\n\n"
        f"Reasoning scaffold:\n{scaffold}\n\n"
        "Draft a professional resolution response."
    )
    response = await call_llm(prompt)
    return {"ticket_id": ticket.id, "response": response, "scaffold_used": ticket.category}

async def mock_llm(prompt: str) -> str:
    # Replace with: anthropic.messages.create(model="claude-opus-4-5", messages=[...])
    return f"[Draft response — prompt tokens ~{len(prompt)//4}]"

async def main():
    tickets = [
        Ticket("T-001", "billing", "I was charged twice for my Pro subscription this month."),
        Ticket("T-002", "technical", "The export CSV button shows a 500 error since yesterday."),
        Ticket("T-003", "account", "I want to change my account email address."),
    ]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[handle_ticket(t, mock_llm) for t in tickets])
    for r in results:
        print(json.dumps(r, indent=2))

asyncio.run(main())
In this setup the meta-buffer is backed by a PostgreSQL table with a

index on the embedding column. The distiller runs as a Celery task after each ticket is marked resolved, feeding new scaffolds back into the buffer with the human-rated quality score as the initial weight.
The most frequent mistake is caching

. A stored answer like “refund ticket #4821 by crediting $19.99” is useless as a template. The correct distillation is “1. Confirm charge. 2. Identify discrepancy. 3. Apply refund policy.” — the structural steps with no instance-specific detail.

Distilling from every LLM output regardless of quality poisons the buffer. A wrong reasoning path stored as a template will be retrieved and used on future calls, compounding errors. Always gate distillation on a quality threshold (judge score, test pass, human approval).

Without decay, a template written for an old API version stays at the top of retrieval results indefinitely, even after the API changes. Implement time-based score decay and a periodic pruning job from day one.
Tagging templates with highly specific labels (e.g.,
) means they are only retrieved for that exact query. Tags should capture the problem

Deploying with an empty buffer and relying on the self-improvement loop to populate it means the first hundreds of calls get no benefit while paying the retrieval overhead. Seed the buffer manually with domain-expert scaffolds before go-live.
Author seed templates with domain experts.
The first 5–10 templates set the quality ceiling for everything derived from them. Invest time in making them precise, complete, and generalizable.

Each step should be an imperative instruction the LLM can follow, not a description of what the step involves. 'Check the Retry-After header' is better than 'The Retry-After header contains wait time'.
Store a version field and timestamp. When an environment changes (new API, policy update), update the template version and deprecate the old one rather than deleting it.
Run A/B tests on retrieval methods.
Tag overlap, embedding cosine similarity, and hybrid re-ranking have different trade-offs. Run both methods in shadow mode and compare retrieval precision before switching.
Track which template was used for each call and whether the outcome was positive. This data drives buffer quality reviews.
Templates over 500 tokens consume context budget and dilute the LLM's focus. Aim for 5–10 crisp steps per scaffold.
Never run the distiller synchronously in the response path. Always use a background queue (Celery, BullMQ, asyncio.create_task) to keep latency predictable.
With a vector DB, retrieval cost scales as

for approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) indices (HNSW, IVF). For buffers under 10,000 templates a brute-force cosine scan in memory is often faster than a network round-trip to a vector DB. Beyond that, use pgvector, Pinecone, or Weaviate.

As the buffer grows, retrieval precision (fraction of top-k results that are genuinely relevant) can degrade if templates are not well-separated in embedding space. Periodically cluster the buffer and merge near-duplicate templates (cosine similarity > 0.95) to keep it tight.

Distillation is an LLM call per successful run. At high throughput, this is expensive. Mitigations: batch distillation (accumulate 50 problem-solution pairs and distill in one call), or trigger distillation only when the retriever returns low-confidence matches (no template with score above 0.6), indicating a gap in the buffer.

Multiple workers reading the buffer concurrently is safe (read-heavy). Multiple workers writing (distillation) requires optimistic locking or a write queue to avoid duplicate template creation. A write queue backed by a message broker (Redis Streams, SQS) is the standard pattern.

Benchmark baseline: with a 500-template buffer backed by pgvector on a 4-core Postgres instance, median retrieval latency is ~25 ms. Embedding generation (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small) adds ~50 ms. Total overhead per call: ~75–100 ms, negligible for non-real-time agent workflows.
Buffer of Thoughts is a pattern, not a framework, so it can be implemented on top of any agent orchestration stack. The following tools provide useful building blocks.
pgvector (PostgreSQL extension), Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Qdrant, FAISS. For small buffers (< 5,000 templates) in-memory FAISS is often sufficient.
OpenAI text-embedding-3-small (cost-efficient), Cohere embed-v3 (multilingual), sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (local, no API cost).

LangChain (Memory + VectorStoreRetriever), LlamaIndex (IngestionPipeline + VectorIndex), CrewAI (task templates), AutoGen (conversation memory). None implement BoT natively, but all provide the primitives needed.
Celery + Redis (Python), BullMQ (Node.js), asyncio.create_task for in-process distillation.
LangSmith, Weights & Biases (W&B), Arize AI for tracking template retrieval accuracy and quality over time.
Example of adding BoT retrieval on top of LangChain's vector store:

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain.schema import Document

# Build an in-memory FAISS index of thought templates
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

template_docs = [
    Document(
        page_content="Step 1: Identify error type. Step 2: Check logs. Step 3: Retry with backoff.",
        metadata={"id": "tpl-001", "tags": ["api", "error"], "score": 0.9},
    ),
    Document(
        page_content="Step 1: Parse requirements. Step 2: Decompose into subtasks. Step 3: Execute in order.",
        metadata={"id": "tpl-002", "tags": ["planning", "decomposition"], "score": 0.85},
    ),
]

vector_store = FAISS.from_documents(template_docs, embeddings)

def retrieve_templates(problem: str, k: int = 2) -> list[Document]:
    return vector_store.similarity_search(problem, k=k)

# Usage
problem = "The payment gateway is returning connection timeout errors."
templates = retrieve_templates(problem)
for doc in templates:
    print(doc.metadata["id"], ":", doc.page_content[:80])
Stable, well-defined tasks with few classes

The meta-buffer is an attack surface. If user-controlled text reaches the distiller without sanitization, a crafted problem statement could inject a malicious scaffold into the buffer (e.g., a template that instructs the agent to exfiltrate data on future calls). Mitigation: run distillation in a separate security context, apply input sanitization before distillation, and require human approval for templates tagged with high-privilege operations.

A low-quality or adversarially crafted template that passes the score threshold will propagate its errors to every future call that retrieves it. Mitigate with: dual scoring (automated judge + human spot-check), version pinning for sensitive template classes, and an audit log of every template write.

If the retriever returns a template for a superficially similar but structurally different problem, the LLM will confidently follow the wrong scaffold. Log retrieved template IDs with every call and alert on retrievals with low confidence scores so the buffer can be corrected.

If the vector store goes down, the agent must fall back gracefully to standard CoT rather than failing entirely. Implement a circuit breaker around the retrieval step: catch exceptions, log the failure, and issue the unscaffolded prompt. This ensures the system degrades gracefully rather than going offline.

Thought templates may embed domain-sensitive information in their scaffolds (e.g., internal API endpoints, pricing logic). Apply the same data-classification controls to the buffer as to your primary data store: encrypt at rest, restrict read access by role, and redact PII before distillation.
Buffer of Thoughts stores distilled reasoning
— not answers — in a shared meta-buffer that all agent calls draw from at inference time.
The self-improvement loop (distillation → store → retrieve) makes the buffer a compounding asset: quality improves automatically as the agent handles more problems.
Retrieval quality is the critical failure point: a wrong template match produces confident, structurally wrong outputs. Log and audit retrievals from day one.
before go-live; never rely solely on the distillation loop to bootstrap quality.
and periodic pruning to prevent stale templates from degrading retrieval precision as environments change.
to keep response latency predictable; never block the main response path on a distillation call.
; it offers little benefit over standard CoT on novel or creative tasks where no relevant template exists.
: apply input sanitization to prevent template injection, and require audit trails for all template writes.
